title: Rewiring an E3 ligase enhances cold resilience and phosphate use in maize
author: Huan Liao
fullText:
Data availabilityGene sequences and amino acid sequences used in this study were collected from MaizeGDB (maize; https://www.maizegdb.org/), Gramene (rice; http://ensembl.gramene.org/index.html) and TAIR10 (Arabidopsis; https://www.arabidopsis.org/). The co-expression data used in this study are available from ATTED-II (https://atted.jp/). The maize and teosinte genotype data were sourced from the HapMap3 resource74, which is publicly accessible through the Panzea database (www.panzea.org). The geographical distribution map was generated using the R packages maps v.3.4.0 and ggplot2 v.3.4.2. MS-based proteomics and ubiquitinomics datasets have been deposited in the Integrated Proteome Resources (iProX) database under accession code IPX0011717000. RNA-sequencing data generated in this paper are accessible through the National Genomics Data Center (NGDC) under the BioProject accession number PRJCA041350 and raw data accession number CRA026553. Uncropped gel and blot images are provided in Supplementary Fig. 1. Source data are provided with this paper.ReferencesBravo-F, P. & Uribe, E. G. H.L. performed most of the experiments and data analyses. Zhen Li designed and X. Zhao performed the ubiquitinomic experiments. K.R., Zhuoyang Li and Z. Liu performed association analyses. Z. Liu and T.S. evaluated cold tolerance and Pi uptake in the F2 population. J.Z. performed maize transformation. X.Y. and F.T. provided maize panels for GWAS and landrace populations. L.G. analysed the geographical distribution of natural variations. W.S. performed structural modelling. Z.Z. performed selection analysis. D.F. modified the model. Z.G. provided advice during manuscript preparation. H.L., Y.S. and S.Y. wrote and revised the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final version.  Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended data figures and tablesExtended Data Fig. 1 Phylogenetic and structural classification of SPX proteins in maize, rice and Arabidopsis, and functional characterization of NLA genes.a, Phylogenetic tree of SPX-domain-containing proteins from Zea mays, Oryza sativa, and Arabidopsis thaliana constructed using full-length protein sequences. b, Predicted structural models of representative proteins (SPX2/SPX-MFS1/NLA/SPX-EXS1) from each SPX domain-containing subfamily, illustrating domain architecture and classification. c, Schematic of mutations in nla-1 and nla-2 alleles. d, Morphological phenotypes of wild-type (LH244), nla-1 and nla-2 plants under normal growth conditions. e,f, Representative chilling phenotypes (e) and quantification of uninjured leaf area (f) of wild-type and F1 hybrids between nla-1 and nla-2 after 4 °C treatment. Scale bar, 5 cm. g, Shoot phosphorus (P) content reduction of wild-type and nla mutants exposed or not to 8 °C for 20 days described in Fig. 1f. h, Expression of NLA in response to cold stress by RT–qPCR. i, Expression of NLA–GFP in overexpression lines driven by the Ubi promoter under cold stress. j, In vitro ubiquitin assay showing E3 ligase activity of NLA. In d–i, data are means ± s.d. (n = 3 independent biological replicates); significance was determined using two-tailed Student’s t-tests (*P < 0.05, **P  < 0.01); ns, not significant.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 2 Transcriptomic and ubiquitinomic profiling reveals NLA-regulated cold-responsive pathways.a, Experimental design for identification of NLA-regulated signalling under cold stress. b, Volcano plots for DEGs under 4 °C vs. 25 °C (left) and between wild-type and nla-1 at 4 °C and 25 °C (right). Significantly regulated genes (Q < 0.05) are labelled. c, Venn diagrams showing overlap of cold-responsive (COR) genes and NLA-regulated genes; overlapping sets were subjected to GO enrichment (see Supplementary Table 4). d, Volcano plots of differentially ubiquitinated K-GG peptides under the same conditions; significantly regulated peptides (Q < 0.05) are labelled. e, Overlap of cold- and NLA-regulated K-GG peptides; overlapping peptides used for GO enrichment (see Supplementary Table 4). f,g, Heat map of 2,610 NLA-regulated DEGs (f) and 3,756 NLA-regulated DUPs (g) clustered by co-expression and annotated with enriched GO terms. Colour scale indicates z-score-normalized log-transformed TPM values.Extended Data Fig. 3 NLA modulates JA-responsive gene expression under cold stress.a, Integrative GO analysis of transcriptomic and ubiquitinomic datasets reveals co-regulated pathways. b, Bubble plot depicting enrichment of 15 major signalling pathways identified in (a). Colour intensity represents –log10(P value). c, Overlap between NLA-regulated COR genes and JA-responsive genes. d, Heat map showing expression of JA-related transcripts in wild-type and nla mutants after 12 h of cold treatment. e, Mutations in lox8 alleles. f,g, Representative chilling phenotypes (f) and quantification of uninjured leaf area (g) of lox8 mutants after 4 °C treatment. Scale bar, 5 cm. h, Expression of BX11 in overexpression lines by RT–qPCR. i,j, Representative chilling phenotypes (i) and quantification of uninjured leaf area (j) of wild-type and BX11-overexpressing seedlings after 4 °C treatment. Scale bar, 5 cm. In g,h,j, data are means ± s.d. (n = 3 independent biological replicates); significance was determined using two-tailed Student’s t-tests.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 4 NLA promotes JAZ11 degradation and JA-mediated cold tolerance.a, Phylogenetic analysis of JAZ proteins, grouped into six clades (I–VI). b, Schematics of truncated JAZ11, JAZ25, and JAZ13 constructs used in yeast two-hybrid assays. c, Yeast two-hybrid assays showing interactions between NLA and full-length or truncated JAZ proteins. Empty vector (EV) was used as a negative control. Yeast was grown on –LW (–Trp/–Leu), –LWHA (–Trp/–Leu/–His/–Ade) synthetic dropout medium.Yeast cells were co-transformed with the indicated pairwise combinations of pGADT7 and pGBKT7 fusion constructs. d, Validation of the efficacy of JAZ11 antibody. e,f, NLA promotes JAZ11 degradation in maize protoplasts (blot (e) and quantification (f)). Different amounts of the empty MYC vector were included to maintain a constant total amount of plasmid DNA (in μg) across all transformation samples. g, Expression of JAZ11 in overexpression lines by RT–qPCR. h,i, Representative chilling phenotypes (h) and quantification of uninjured leaf area (i) of JAZ11-overexpressing seedlings after 4 °C treatment. Scale bar, 5 cm. j, Mutations in jaz11 knockout alleles. k,l, Representative chilling phenotypes (k) and quantification of uninjured leaf area (l) of jaz11 mutants. Scale bar, 5 cm. m, Expression of AOS2b, LOX8, LOX13, and BX10-BX12 in JAZ11-overexpression plants after 12 h of 4 °C treatment. In f,g,i,l,m, data are means ± s.d. (n = 3 independent biological replicates); significance was determined using two-tailed Student’s t-tests.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 5 Natural variation in JAZ11 is associated with maize cold tolerance.a, Candidate gene association analysis identifies SNP-379 (A-to-G) in JAZ11 coding region causing a K47R substitution significantly correlated with cold tolerance. b, LC–MS analysis confirming K47 as a ubiquitination site. c–f, The K47R variant stabilizes JAZ11 protein in cell-free degradation (c,d) and maize protoplast assays (e,f). In e,f, different amounts of the empty MYC vector were included to maintain a constant total amount of plasmid DNA (in μg) across all transformation samples. In a, data are means ± s.d. n denotes the number of inbred lines with each haplotype (n = 98 for hap 379 G; n = 7 for hap 379 A). In d,f, data are means ± s.d. (n = 3 independent biological replicates); significance was determined using two-tailed Student’s t-tests (*P <  0.05, ***P < 0.001); ns, not significant.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 6 Regulation of Pi uptake by PT4 variants and InsP-dependent targeting by NLA.a, Population structure of 192 maize inbred lines classified as TST (tropical/subtropical), NSS (temperate), SS (B73-derived), or mixed. b, Quantile-Quantile (Q–Q) plot of GWAS for shoot P content under 8 °C treatment for two weeks. c–f, K267A substitution enhances PT4 protein stability in cell-free (c,d) and maize protoplast (e,f) degradation assays. In e,f, different amounts of the empty MYC vector were included to maintain a constant total amount of plasmid DNA (in μg) across all transformation samples. g,h, Distribution of shoot P content (g) and relative leaf injury (h) across 192 inbred lines after cold treatment. i, Scatter plot showing no correlation between shoot P content and leaf injury after cold stress (R2 = 0.014). j, Expression of PT4 in overexpression lines by RT–qPCR. k,l, Representative chilling phenotypes (k) and quantification of relative uninjured leaf area (l) of wild-type and PT4-overexpressing seedlings after 4oC treatment. Scale bar, 5 cm. m, Shoot P contents of 14-d-old PT4-overexpressing seedlings after 4 days at 4 °C. n, Cold-induced expression of PT4 in wild-type under 4 °C treatment. o–r, Co-IP showing in vivo interaction of NLA with PT4 (o,p) and JAZ11 (q,r) under varying InsP6 concentrations. s, Yeast two-hybrid assays of JAZ11 interaction with full-length or SPX-domain-deleted NLA constructs. Empty vector (EV) was used as a negative control. Yeast was grown on –LW (–Trp/–Leu), –LWHA (–Trp/–Leu/–His/–Ade) synthetic dropout medium.Yeast cells were co-transformed with the indicated pairwise combinations of pGADT7 and pGBKT7 fusion constructs. In d,f,j,l-n,p,r, data are means ± s.d. (n = 3 independent biological replicates; significance was determined using a two-tailed Student’s t-test (*P < 0.05); ns, not significant.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 7 AI-assisted genome editing of NLA enhances cold tolerance.a, Molecular docking of InsP6-NLA (left) and InsP8-NLA (right) complexes. Key residues in SPX domain involved in polar interactions are shown; PBC and lysine surface cluster (KSC) are coloured in green and blue, respectively. b, Gene structure of NLA showing CRISPR/Cas targeted sites indicated (exon in yellow). Selected guide RNA (gRNA) sequences are highlighted in green. In i, significance was determined by two-tailed Student’s t-tests. n represent independent biological replicates.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 10 Model illustrating the functions of NLA and the AI-engineered NLAΔ12 in coordinating cold tolerance, PUE and yield stability in maize.Under cold stress, NLA accumulation enhances the degradation of JAZ11 and PT4 via InsP-independent and InsP-dependent pathways, respectively, thereby activating JA signalling to reinforce cold tolerance while concurrently restricting Pi uptake. The AI-engineered NLA variant, NLAΔ12, carrying a four–amino acid deletion, selectively disrupts NLA-mediated Pi sensing and thus impairs downstream impairing PT4 regulation, while strengthening JA signalling output. Consequently, NLAΔ12 confers enhanced cold resilience and improved PUE, contributing to yield stability under cold-prone environments. Arrow thickness in the model reflects the relative strength of regulatory or signalling outputs. Created in BioRender. Yang, S.
